content:
Accessibility

Web Development

11 min read
1 hour ago

--

“Make it accessible” is easy to say and strangely hard to pin down. WCAG is the standard that turns a good intention into something you can actually check — four principles, three levels, and a testing reality most teams get wrong. A from-scratch walkthrough.
There’s a quiet assumption baked into most websites: that the person on the other end can see the screen clearly, use a mouse, hear the audio, and process everything the way the designer did. For a huge number of people, one or more of those assumptions is simply false. They’re navigating by keyboard because a mouse isn’t an option. They’re listening to the page through a screen reader. They’re zoomed to 300% because that’s what makes text legible. They’re on a phone in bright sun with a cracked screen and shaky hands.
Accessibility is what decides whether the web works for those people too — and WCAG is how we stop hand-waving about it and start measuring it. The Web Content Accessibility Guidelines are the closest thing the field has to a shared, testable definition of “accessible.” If you build for the web and you’ve ever been told to “make it WCAG compliant” without being told what that actually means, this one’s for you.
I’ll build it up from the ground — what it is, how it’s structured, and, crucially, the part about testing that trips almost everyone up.

First, what WCAG actually is
WCAG is a set of guidelines published by the W3C (the standards body that governs the web) through its Web Accessibility Initiative. It’s not a law and it’s not a piece of software. It’s a specification — a carefully worded, versioned document that defines what a web page must do to be usable by people with a wide range of disabilities: visual, auditory, motor, cognitive, and more.
It matters for two reasons, and it’s worth being honest that they’re different. The first is the real one: a meaningful slice of your users cannot use a site that ignores accessibility, and they are people who deserve to book the appointment, read the article, or complete the checkout like anyone else. The second is the practical lever: accessibility is increasingly a legal requirement, and WCAG — specifically Level AA — is the benchmark that regulations and courts around the world point to. Do it because it’s right; the fact that it’s also required just removes your excuses.
The guidelines have evolved. WCAG 2.0 landed in 2008 and set the foundation. WCAG 2.1 (2018) added criteria for mobile, low vision, and cognitive needs. WCAG 2.2, published in October 2023, is the current recommendation and adds a further set of criteria — things like minimum target sizes and consistent help. Each version is backward-compatible: meet 2.2 and you’ve met 2.1 and 2.0. When someone says “WCAG” today, aim at 2.2 AA and you’re on solid ground.

The heart of it: the four POUR principles
Everything in WCAG hangs off four principles, and they spell a usefully memorable acronym: POUR — Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust. If you understand these four, you understand the intent behind every single rule, which matters far more than memorizing the rules themselves.
Perceivable. Users must be able to perceive the information — it can’t be invisible to all of their senses. If content is conveyed only one way, anyone who can’t use that channel is locked out. So images need text alternatives (alt text) that a screen reader can announce. Videos need captions for people who can't hear them. Text needs sufficient color contrast so people with low vision can read it. And information must never be carried by color alone — a form field that shows errors only by turning red is invisible to a colorblind user.
Operable. Users must be able to operate the interface — every control, every menu, every flow. The classic test: can you use the entire site with only a keyboard, no mouse? A huge population navigates that way, and a custom dropdown that only responds to clicks quietly excludes all of them. Operable also means giving people enough time (don’t expire a form in twenty seconds), not designing anything that flashes in a way that can trigger seizures, and providing a visible focus indicator so keyboard users can see where they are on the page.
Understandable. Users must be able to understand both the content and the interface. Text should be readable and written as plainly as the subject allows. The interface should be predictable — things behave consistently, and selecting an option doesn’t trigger some surprising context change. And when a user makes a mistake, the site should help them fix it: clear error messages that say what went wrong and how to correct it, not a generic “invalid input.”
Robust. The content must be robust enough to work reliably with the tools people use to access it — especially assistive technologies like screen readers and voice control. In practice this means using clean, valid, standards-based markup and proper semantics: a real <button> element instead of a <div> dressed up to look like one, correct labels on form fields, correct roles on custom components. Robust is the principle that makes the other three survive contact with the messy reality of real browsers and real screen readers.
Four principles. Every rule in the entire standard is just a specific, testable expression of one of them.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

How the standard is actually structured
WCAG is organized as a hierarchy, and seeing the shape makes the whole thing far less intimidating.
At the top are the four principles (POUR). Under each principle sit guidelines — broad goals, like “provide text alternatives” or “make all functionality available from a keyboard.” There are thirteen of these. And under the guidelines sit the success criteria — the specific, testable statements that you actually measure against. Each success criterion is written so you can give a clear pass or fail: does this page meet it, yes or no? That testability is the entire point. “Be accessible” is an argument; “all images have appropriate text alternatives” is a check.
Below the success criteria, the W3C also publishes techniques — specific, non-mandatory ways to satisfy a criterion (how to do it in HTML, in ARIA, and so on). Techniques are advice, not requirements; you can meet a criterion any way that works. The success criteria are what conformance is judged on.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The three levels: A, AA, and AAA
Every success criterion is assigned a conformance level, and this is where the practical decisions get made. There are three, and they’re cumulative.
Level A is the floor — the most essential requirements, addressing the barriers that would otherwise make a site completely unusable for some people. Things like keyboard access and text alternatives for images. Fail these and the site is broken for whole groups of users.
Level AA is the real-world target. It builds on A and adds the criteria that make a site genuinely usable for the large majority — sufficient color contrast, text that reflows when zoomed, consistent navigation, meaningful labels. AA is the level that regulations and legal standards around the world overwhelmingly point to. When a client says “make it compliant,” they almost always mean AA, whether or not they know it.
Level AAA is the highest bar — the gold standard, with the strictest criteria (even higher contrast, sign-language interpretation for video, and more). It’s excellent to aim for where you can, but the W3C itself notes that it isn’t reasonable to require AAA across all content for entire sites. Treat AAA as aspiration, AA as the goal, A as the non-negotiable minimum.
So the honest summary most teams need: target AA. Meet A completely on the way there, and reach for AAA where it’s practical.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A taste of what WCAG 2.2 added
Because 2.2 is current, a couple of its newer criteria are worth knowing, since they reflect where accessibility thinking has moved: toward touch, motor differences, and not making people re-do work.
Newer criteria include a minimum target size for touch controls (so buttons aren’t so tiny that people with motor difficulties or big thumbs can’t hit them), a requirement that a strong focus indicator isn’t hidden behind other elements, alternatives to dragging movements (which are hard for many users), and consistent help — putting support links in the same place across pages. None of these are exotic. They’re the accumulated lessons of watching real people struggle with real interfaces.

The part everyone gets wrong: testing
Here’s where I want to slow down, because it’s the single most common misunderstanding I run into. People assume accessibility is a thing you can fully verify with an automated tool — run a scan, get a green check, done. It isn’t, and believing it is gives you a false sense of safety that’s arguably worse than knowing you haven’t tested.
The reality is that accessibility testing has two halves, and you need both.
Automated testing uses a tool (often built on an engine like the open-source axe-core) to scan a page’s code against the machine-checkable success criteria. It’s fast, it’s repeatable, and it’s genuinely valuable — it will reliably catch missing alt attributes, insufficient color contrast, missing form labels, empty buttons, broken heading structures, and a long list of other concrete defects. If you're not running automated checks, you're leaving easy, obvious wins on the table.
But automated tools can only ever check what a machine can judge. They cannot tell you whether your alt text is meaningful ("image123.jpg" passes the presence check and helps no one), whether your page's reading order makes sense, whether a keyboard user can actually complete your checkout flow, whether your focus indicator is visible, or whether a screen reader announces your custom modal in a way a human can follow. Those require manual testing — a person using the keyboard alone, a person listening with NVDA or VoiceOver, a person zooming to 200% and checking nothing breaks.
The trap is treating a clean automated scan as “we’re accessible.” A clean scan means you’ve cleared the machine-detectable issues — a necessary first pass, not a finish line. The honest model is: automate the mechanical layer so humans are free to test the things only humans can judge.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The wrinkle people miss: accessibility isn’t one screen size
There’s a second reality that catches teams off guard, and it’s the one that pushed me to build a tool. Accessibility isn’t a fixed property of a page — it changes with the viewport. The same page can pass on a desktop and fail badly on a phone.
Think about why. Responsive layouts reflow content at different widths, so a color-contrast problem might only appear where a background image shifts on mobile. Touch targets that are comfortably spaced on a laptop can end up cramped and overlapping on a small screen. A menu that’s perfectly operable as a desktop navigation bar can collapse into a hamburger that traps keyboard focus. Content that’s hidden or reordered at one breakpoint can wreck the reading order at another. If you only ever test the desktop view — which is exactly what most quick checks do — you’re blind to an entire class of issues that only your mobile and tablet users will ever hit.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Why I ended up building a multi-device accessibility scanner
Those two truths collided for me repeatedly. First: automated scanning is a valuable first pass but only if you actually do it. Second: accessibility issues hide at specific screen sizes, so scanning one viewport quietly misses a lot. And doing a proper multi-device automated pass by hand — resizing the browser to phone, tablet, laptop, and desktop, running the checker at each, screenshotting the failures — is exactly the k
